skill.name: psql
skill.description: Run PostgreSQL queries, meta-commands, and SQL files through the psql command-line client.
skill.summary: Use psql from an AI coding session to inspect and query PostgreSQL databases.
skill.author: Bind

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, credential, and heuristic findings. Review confirms the command and credential themes are real for a psql database skill, but most individual hits are markdown examples, documentation links, or false positives rather than malicious behavior. The skill should publish only with a clear warning because it can run SQL and SQL files against credentialed PostgreSQL databases.

medium_findings.title: Credentialed PostgreSQL Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run SQL queries and psql meta-commands through the psql CLI using PostgreSQL connection variables, including PGPASSWORD. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it can expose or modify database data if used with broad credentials.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented workflow directly uses psql with database credentials and arbitrary SQL text. This confirms a real operational risk, although it aligns with the advertised database skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: SQL File Execution From Workspace Paths
medium_findings.description: The skill supports executing SQL files from paths in the workspace. This is useful for migrations and seed data, but it can run destructive statements if the file contents are unreviewed.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The --file option and examples clearly show SQL files being executed. The risk depends on user permissions and file contents, so medium severity is appropriate.

low_findings.title: Documentation Links Flagged as Network Access
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are prerequisite links to bun and PostgreSQL documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs or performs network requests beyond normal database connectivity implied by psql.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in markdown prerequisite bullets. There is no code or instruction that posts credentials or query output to these links.

low_findings.title: Environment File References Are Configuration Guidance
low_findings.description: The skill mentions a .env file and shows PostgreSQL variable names. This is sensitive configuration guidance, but no evidence found that the skill reads hidden files or exfiltrates environment contents.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines clearly document PGHOST, PGUSER, and PGPASSWORD usage. The directory does not include executable code showing hidden file reads, so the remaining concern is operational handling of secrets.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptography and reconnaissance patterns in ordinary markdown text and SQL examples. No evidence found of cryptographic code, host scanning, or system reconnaissance commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found SQL examples and headings at the cited lines, not crypto APIs or system reconnaissance behavior. The findings appear caused by pattern matching against documentation text.

dangerous_patterns.title: External psql Invocation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly documents running bun to invoke a psql query helper. External command execution is central to the skill and should be limited to trusted SQL and least-privilege database users.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is explicitly documented across examples. It is not malicious by itself, but it enables database operations with the active credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database Secret Handling Through Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill expects PostgreSQL connection secrets in exported variables or a .env file. Users should avoid privileged accounts and should not ask the assistant to print secret values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: PGPASSWORD and related variables are explicitly documented. The risk is credential exposure or misuse, not confirmed exfiltration.

content.user_title: Run PostgreSQL Queries From Your AI Workspace
content.value_statement: Database work often needs quick schema checks, counts, and administrative queries during development. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a documented psql workflow for direct PostgreSQL inspection.
actual_capabilities: Runs PostgreSQL SQL queries through the psql command-line client.
Supports psql meta-commands for listing tables, indexes, databases, and table definitions.
Executes SQL files for migrations, seed data, or scripted database tasks.
Provides tuple-only output for workflows that parse query results.
Supports longer query timeouts for slow analytical or administrative queries.
Can request JSON-wrapped output when downstream tools need structured results.
limitations: Requires bun, psql, and valid PostgreSQL connection variables before use.
Does not maintain a persistent database connection between queries.
Can run destructive SQL if the user or file provides destructive statements.
The reviewed directory documents an entry path, but the executable implementation is not present here.

use_cases.title: Inspect a Development Database
use_cases.description: List tables, describe schemas, and run small checks while debugging application behavior.

use_cases.title: Check Operational State
use_cases.description: Review active connections, table sizes, and recent records during routine database maintenance.

use_cases.title: Run Controlled Data Tasks
use_cases.description: Execute read-only aggregations or approved SQL files to answer product and reporting questions.

prompt_templates.title: List Database Tables
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the psql skill to list the tables in my PostgreSQL database. Do not change any data.

prompt_templates.title: Describe One Table
prompt_templates.scenario: Understand a table before editing code
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the psql skill to describe the users table and summarize the columns, indexes, and key relationships.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Read-Only Count
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the psql skill to count pending orders grouped by status. Only run read-only SQL.

prompt_templates.title: Review and Execute a Migration
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the migration file first, explain the expected changes, then use the psql skill to run it only after confirmation.

output_examples.input: List the tables in the current PostgreSQL database.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs the table listing command and leaves the terminal output visible for direct inspection.

output_examples.input: Check how many orders are pending.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs a read-only count query and reports the count without exposing connection secrets.
The assistant reviews the migration intent, asks for confirmation, and then executes the approved file.

best_practices: Use a least-privilege PostgreSQL user, preferably read-only for exploration and reporting.
Review SQL files before execution, especially migrations and seed scripts.
Keep database passwords out of prompts, logs, screenshots, and shared terminal output.
anti_patterns: Do not run unreviewed SQL files from unknown branches or generated sources.
Do not use production administrator credentials for routine schema inspection.
Do not ask the assistant to print or summarize environment variable values.

faq.question: Does this skill require PostgreSQL tools installed locally?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow requires both bun and the psql client.

faq.question: Can it run psql meta-commands?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation includes meta-commands for tables, indexes, databases, and table details.

faq.question: Can it execute SQL files?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports a file option for migrations, seed data, and other SQL scripts.

faq.question: Is it safe for production databases?
faq.answer: Use caution. Publish with a warning and use least-privilege credentials for any production access.

faq.question: Does it store database credentials?
faq.answer: No storage behavior is shown in the reviewed files. The documentation expects credentials in environment variables or a .env file.

faq.question: Does it change query output formatting?
faq.answer: The documentation says terminal output should remain visible and not be re-summarized by default.
